revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ipetam]][[ja:イペタム]]
{{No footnotes|date=2019-10-25}} '''Ipetamu''' ({{ill|Ipetam|ja|イペタム|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipetam|qq=}}; Ainu Romanized transcription: ipetam)<ref>ムは[[小書きム]]</ref> is a legendary {{ill|demon sword|ja|妖刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/demon sword|1=cursed sword|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11446778}} from the folklore of the {{ill|Ainu people|en|Ainu people|qid=Q101828|lt=Ainu|1=Ainu people}} people in {{ill|Hokkaido|en|Hokkaido|qid=Q35581|lt=Hokkaido|1=Hokkaido}}.<ref name=d065817_d>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.asahikawa.hokkaido.jp/hakubutukan/kenkyuu/d065817_d/fil/30-1-1.pdf|format=PDF|title=第19回旭川市アイヌ語地名表記推進懇談会の開催結果|publisher=旭川市博物館|date=2018-pp-08|accessdate=2023-12-21|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20200202144849/https://www.city.asahikawa.hokkaido.jp/hakubutukan/kenkyuu/d065817_d/fil/30-1-1.pdf|archivedate=2020-02-02}}</ref>
Alternate spellings include '''Epetamu'''<ref>北海道伝説集 アイヌ篇、258頁</ref>, '''Ebetamu'''<ref name="日高村五拾年史">日高村五拾年史、54頁</ref>, '''Ibetamu'''<ref name="ユーカラ概説">ユーカラ概説、42頁</ref>, and '''Ibetan'''<ref>サルウンクル物語、14-15頁</ref>.
== Overview ==
The Ipetamu is described as a sword that cannot be sheathed once drawn until it has drawn blood, and it is said to fly through the air to kill people on its own.<ref name=アイヌ語辞典>萱野茂のアイヌ語辞典、71頁</ref><ref name=アイヌ語沙流方言辞典>アイヌ語沙流方言辞典、693頁</ref> The word derives from the Ainu language: *ipe* ("eat") and *tam* ("sword").<ref name="d065817_d" /><ref name="アイヌ語辞典" />
The term "Ipetamu" serves as a general noun for "demon sword" (人喰い刀), and similar legends are found throughout Hokkaido, often naming individual swords such as {{ill|Pinnemosomi|ja|ピンネモソミ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pinnemosomi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Abashiri|en|Abashiri|qid=Q305640|lt=Abashiri|1=Abashiri}}, {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|en|Moyoro Shell Mound|qid=Q11344562|lt=Moyoro Shell Mound|1=Moyoro Shell Mound}}; {{ill|Mannemosomi|ja|マッネモソミ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannemosomi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Bihoro, Hokkaido|en|Bihoro, Hokkaido|qid=Q1355074|lt=Bihoro|1=Bihoro, Hokkaido}}; and {{ill|Opokorope|ja|オポコロペ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Opokorope|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Kushiro|en|Kushiro|qid=Q271917|lt=Kushiro|1=Kushiro}}, each associated with unique yet sinister traits.
While Ipetamu is primarily a legendary weapon, the actual {{ill|Ainu swords|ja|アイヌ刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainu swords|1=Ainu sword|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11282345}} often take the form of Japanese {{ill|Tachi|en|Tachi|qid=Q120676|lt=tachi|1=Tachi}} or {{ill|Tantō|en|Tantō|qid=Q654077|lt=wakizashi|1=Tantō}}, with decorations preferred by the Ainu people.
The distinction between "Ipetamu" and "Epetamu" in written records is thought to stem from dialectical variations or researchers' inability to distinguish between "I" and "E" sounds, influenced by certain Japanese regional dialects.
Additional tales also feature {{ill|Ipeop|ja|イペオプ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipeop|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a spear associated with similar supernatural properties.<ref name="d065817_d" /><ref>萱野茂のアイヌ語辞典、70頁</ref>
== Legends of Demon Swords Across Regions ==
=== Kamikawa === A Kamikawa chief's home contained a soot-covered package hung near the sacred window, with strict instructions from ancestors never to open it. Over time, the package began to glow ominously and caused strange phenomena, killing anyone who encountered its light. Attempts to dispose of the sword by throwing it into rivers or the depths of {{ill|Ishikari River|en|Ishikari River|qid=Q1061515|lt=Ishikari River|1=Ishikari River}} failed, as it always returned. Eventually, a divine message guided the chief to a ritual at a swamp, where the sword was finally cast away. The rock by the swamp became known as {{ill|Epetam-shuma|ja|エペタムシュマ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epetam-shuma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ("Man-eating sword rock").<ref>伝説の旭川及其附近、54-58頁</ref>
=== Saru === In the Saru region, the Ipetamu was kept in a box and would make rattling sounds unless fed animal hides. A misunderstanding led attackers to flee, believing the sound of a loose machete was the feared Ipetamu.<ref name=日高村五拾年史>日高村五拾年史、54頁</ref>
=== Other Regional Legends === Stories of the Ipetamu appear throughout Hokkaido, often linked with conflict, curses, and supernatural phenomena.
== Yūkara Stories of Cursed Swords == The *Yūkara* epic poems also feature demon swords like the Ipetamu, often embodying themes of retribution, betrayal, and purification.<ref>ユーカラ概説、38-42頁</ref>
== References ==
=== Sources === {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Hiroshi Udagawa|en|Hiroshi Udagawa|qid=Q11449583|lt=Udagawa Hiroshi|1=Hiroshi Udagawa}}, ''Ainu Legends and Fortifications,'' Hokkaido Publishing Planning Center, 1981. * {{ill|Kayano Shigeru|en|Kayano Shigeru|qid=Q290222|lt=Kayano Shigeru|1=Kayano Shigeru}}, ''Kayano Shigeru's Ainu Dictionary,'' Sanseido, 2002.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kutune Shirka|en|Kutune Shirka|qid=Q17050522|lt=Kutne-sirika|1=Kutune Shirka}} - Ainu epics featuring legendary swords. * {{ill|Ainu sword|ja|アイヌ刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainu sword|1=Ainu sword|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11282345}} - Ritual swords used in Ainu culture.
== External Links == * [https://www.aozora.gr.jp/cards/001540/files/53886_50777.html Entry on Ainu legends, 青空文庫]
{{translated page|ja|イペタム|version=103851050|comment=Imported full ja history}}
